summary:
Johann Sebastian Bach (1685–1750 AD) was the supreme composer of the Baroque period — a German musician who synthesized the entire European musical tradition of his era into works of incomparable structural complexity and expressive depth, producing a body of music that subsequent generations have regarded as the foundation of Western classical music. Bach spent most of his career as a church organist and court musician in provincial German cities (Arnstadt, Weimar, Köthen, Leipzig), working within the institutional structures of Lutheran church music and aristocratic patronage that shaped Baroque composition. Despite his limited fame in his own lifetime (largely confined to his specialty as an organist), his Mass in B Minor, St. Matthew Passion, Well-Tempered Clavier, Brandenburg Concertos, and hundreds of other works demonstrated a mastery of counterpoint, harmony, and musical architecture that astonished later musicians.

Bach was largely forgotten after his death in 1750, his style considered old-fashioned compared to the galant simplicity fashionable in the Classical period. His rediscovery began with Felix Mendelssohn's performance of the St. Matthew Passion in 1829 — the first performance in nearly 80 years — which triggered a 'Bach revival' that reassessed his music as foundational rather than archaic. The [[event:2437|Baroque period]] in music extended from approximately 1600 to 1750, coinciding with the establishment of the major and minor key system that remains the basis of Western tonal music. Bach's exact contemporary George Frideric Handel, working primarily in England, brought Italian opera and oratorio to British audiences; their contrasting careers — Handel the international celebrity, Bach the provincial craftsman — illustrate the range of musical possibilities in the Baroque era.
